subject: Physics
q:
In an experiment, brass and steel wires of length 1 m each with areas of cross section 1 mm2 are used. The wires are connected in series and one end of the combined wire is connected to a rigid support and other end is subjected to elongation. The stress required to produce a net elongation of 0.2 mm is,
[Given, the Young's Modulus for steel and brass are, respectively, 120×109 N/m2 and 60×109 N/m2 ]
question:
In an experiment, brass and steel wires of length 1 m each with areas of cross section 1 mm2 are used. The wires are connected in series and one end of the combined wire is connected to a rigid support and other end is subjected to elongation. The stress required to produce a net elongation of 0.2 mm is,
[Given, the Young's Modulus for steel and brass are, respectively, 120×109 N/m2 and 60×109 N/m2 ]
solution:
Stress will be same in both rods
Stress =Y1Δl1l=Y2Δl2l … (1)
And
Δl1+Δl2=0.2×10-3 m … (2)
From equation (1) and (2)
Stress ×lY1+lY2=0.2×10-3 m
Stress ×1120×109+160×109=0.2×10-3 m
Stress =8×106 N/m2

subject: Physics
q: An elastic spring under tension of $3 \mathrm{~N}$ has a length $a$. Its length is $b$ under tension $2 \mathrm{~N}$. For its length $(3 a-2 b)$, the value of tension will be______$\mathrm{N}$.
question: An elastic spring under tension of $3 \mathrm{~N}$ has a length $a$. Its length is $b$ under tension $2 \mathrm{~N}$. For its length $(3 a-2 b)$, the value of tension will be______$\mathrm{N}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 3=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{a}-\ell) \\ & 2=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{b}-\ell) \\ & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{K}(3 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}-\ell) \\ & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{K}(3(\mathrm{a}-\ell)-2(\mathrm{~b}-\ell) \\ & =\mathrm{K}\left[3\left(\frac{3}{\mathrm{~K}}\right)-2\left(\frac{2}{\mathrm{~K}}\right)\right] \\ & =9-4 \\ & =5 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform wire of length $l$ of weight $w$ is suspended from the roof with a weight of $W$ at the other end. The stress in the wire at $\dfrac{l}{3}$ distance from the top is $\left(\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}\right)$, where, $A$ is the cross sectional area of the wire. The value of $\gamma$ is _______.
question: A uniform wire of length $l$ of weight $w$ is suspended from the roof with a weight of $W$ at the other end. The stress in the wire at $\dfrac{l}{3}$ distance from the top is $\left(\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}\right)$, where, $A$ is the cross sectional area of the wire. The value of $\gamma$ is _______.
solution:
Let $T$ be the tension in the wire at a distance $\dfrac{l}{3}$ from the top.

The length of the wire below this point is $l - \dfrac{l}{3} = \dfrac{2l}{3}$.

The weight of this lower part of the wire is $\dfrac{w}{l} \times \dfrac{2l}{3} = \dfrac{2w}{3}$.

The total downward force (tension $T$) at this point is the sum of the weight $W$ attached at the bottom and the weight of the lower part of the wire:

$T = W + \dfrac{2w}{3}$

The stress at this point is given by the tension divided by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{T}{A} = \dfrac{W + \dfrac{2w}{3}}{A} = \dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{3}\dfrac{w}{A}$

Comparing this with the given expression for stress, $\dfrac{W}{A} + \dfrac{2}{\gamma}\dfrac{w}{A}$, we get:

$\gamma = 3$

Answer: $3$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped rigidly at one end. When the other end of the wire is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. Another wire of the same material of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>r</mi></math> is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>F</mi></math> under same conditions. The increase in length of this wire is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped rigidly at one end. When the other end of the wire is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. Another wire of the same material of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>r</mi></math> is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>F</mi></math> under same conditions. The increase in length of this wire is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Young's modulus is given by Y=FLA∆L, here, A is cross-section area and ∆L is change in length.

Now, when force F is applied, change in length ΔL1=FLAY=FLπr2Y=5 cm

Now, when force 4F is applied, change in length

ΔL2=4 F4Lπ16r2Y=FLπr2Y=5 cm

subject: Physics
q: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
question: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material is given by

Y=stressstrain=stress∆lL   ...1

The formula to calculate the potential energy U stored into the wire is given by

U=12×stress×strain×volume=12×stress×∆lL×A×L= 12×stress×A×∆l   ...2

From equation (1) and equation (2), it can be written that

U=12×Y×∆l2L×A   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the required cross-sectional area of the wire.

80 J=12×2.0×1011 Nm-2×0.02 m220 m×A⇒A= 2×80 J×202.0×1011×4×10-4 Nm-1=4×10-5 m2×106 mm21 m2= 40 mm2

subject: Physics
q: A steel rod has a radius of 20 mm and a length of 2.0 m. A force of 62.8 kN stretches it along its length. Young's modulus of steel is 2.0×1011N m-2. The longitudinal strain produced in the wire is _________×10-5.
question: A steel rod has a radius of 20 mm and a length of 2.0 m. A force of 62.8 kN stretches it along its length. Young's modulus of steel is 2.0×1011N m-2. The longitudinal strain produced in the wire is _________×10-5.
solution:
Young's modulus is given as

Y=StressStrain=FA∆LL

The given data is 

Stress=62.8 kN

r=20×10-3 m

Y=2×1011 N m-2

Thus,

Strain=FA×Y⇒∆LL=62.8×103 Nπ×20×10-3 m2×2×1011 N m-2=25×10-5 N

subject: Physics
q: If the ratio of lengths, radii and Young's moduli of steel and brass wires in the figure are $a, b$ and $c$ respectively, then the corresponding ratio of increase in their lengths is :
question: If the ratio of lengths, radii and Young's moduli of steel and brass wires in the figure are $a, b$ and $c$ respectively, then the corresponding ratio of increase in their lengths is :
solution:
According to questions,
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{a}, \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{b}, \frac{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}=\mathrm{c}, \frac{\Delta \ell \mathrm{s}}{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=? \\
& \text { As, } \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{F} \ell}{\mathrm{A} \Delta \ell} \Rightarrow \Delta \ell=\frac{\mathrm{F} \ell}{\mathrm{Ay}} \\
& \Delta \ell_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{mg} \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}\left[\because \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{s}}=(\mathrm{M}+2 \mathrm{M}) \mathrm{g}\right] \\
& \Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{Mg}\ell_{\mathrm{b}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}\left[\because \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}=2 \mathrm{Mg}\right]
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\Delta \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}=\frac{\frac{3 \mathrm{Mg} \ell_{\mathrm{s}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{s}}}}{\frac{2 \mathrm{Mg} \cdot \ell_{\mathrm{b}}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{b}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{b}}}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{b}^2 \mathrm{C}}$

subject: Physics
q: A string $A$ of length $0.314$ m and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$ is connected to another string $B$ of length and Young's modulus both twice of those of $A$. This series combination of strings is then suspended from a rigid support and its free end is fixed to a load of mass $0.8$ kg. The net change in length of the combination is _____ mm. (radius of both the strings is $0.2$ mm and acceleration due to gravity $= 10$ m/s$^2$) (Mass of both strings is to be neglected as compared to the mass of load)
question: A string $A$ of length $0.314$ m and Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$ is connected to another string $B$ of length and Young's modulus both twice of those of $A$. This series combination of strings is then suspended from a rigid support and its free end is fixed to a load of mass $0.8$ kg. The net change in length of the combination is _____ mm. (radius of both the strings is $0.2$ mm and acceleration due to gravity $= 10$ m/s$^2$) (Mass of both strings is to be neglected as compared to the mass of load)
solution:
Given:
Length of string A, $L_A = 0.314$ m
Young's modulus of string A, $Y_A = 2 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Radius of both strings, $r = 0.2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
Length of string B, $L_B = 2L_A = 0.628$ m
Young's modulus of string B, $Y_B = 2Y_A = 4 \times 10^{10}$ N/m$^2$
Mass of load, $m = 0.8$ kg
Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$

Tension in both strings, $T = mg = 0.8 \times 10 = 8$ N

Cross-sectional area of both strings:
$A = \pi r^2 = 3.14 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m$^2$

Elongation in string A:
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{3.14 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{10}}$
$\Delta L_A = \dfrac{8 \times 0.314}{8 \times 3.14 \times 10^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{10^2} = 10^{-3}$ m $= 1$ mm

Elongation in string B:
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{T L_B}{A Y_B}$
$\Delta L_B = \dfrac{8 \times (2 \times 0.314)}{A \times (2 Y_A)} = \dfrac{T L_A}{A Y_A} = 1$ mm

Net change in length of the combination:
$\Delta L = \Delta L_A + \Delta L_B = 1 + 1 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two clamps <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart. The resulting strain in the wire is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The lowest frequency of the transverse vibrations in the wire (Young's modulus of wire  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ), (to the nearest integer),_______
question: A wire of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two clamps <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart. The resulting strain in the wire is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The lowest frequency of the transverse vibrations in the wire (Young's modulus of wire  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ), (to the nearest integer),_______
solution:
Fundamental frequency in the string

f=12ℓTμ=12ℓTρA=12ℓYΔℓρℓ
f=12ℓYΔℓρℓ
=129×1010×4.9×10-49×10-3×10-6=35

subject: Physics
q: A metal string $A$ is suspended from a rigid support and its free end is attached to a block of mass $M$. Second block having mass $2M$ is suspended at the bottom of the first block using a string $B$. The area of cross sections of strings $A$ and $B$ are same. The ratio of lengths of strings of $A$ to $B$ is $2$ and the ratio of their Young's moduli $(Y_A/Y_B)$ is $0.5$. The ratio of elongations in $A$ to $B$ is ______.
question: A metal string $A$ is suspended from a rigid support and its free end is attached to a block of mass $M$. Second block having mass $2M$ is suspended at the bottom of the first block using a string $B$. The area of cross sections of strings $A$ and $B$ are same. The ratio of lengths of strings of $A$ to $B$ is $2$ and the ratio of their Young's moduli $(Y_A/Y_B)$ is $0.5$. The ratio of elongations in $A$ to $B$ is ______.
solution:
Tension in string $B$ is due to the block of mass $2M$:

$T_B = 2Mg$

Tension in string $A$ is due to both blocks of mass $M$ and $2M$:

$T_A = Mg + 2Mg = 3Mg$

The elongation in a string is given by Hooke's law as $\Delta L = \dfrac{TL}{AY}$.

The ratio of elongations in string $A$ to string $B$ is:

$\dfrac{\Delta L_A}{\Delta L_B} = \left(\dfrac{T_A}{T_B}\right) \left(\dfrac{L_A}{L_B}\right) \left(\dfrac{A_B}{A_A}\right) \left(\dfrac{Y_B}{Y_A}\right)$

Given that $\dfrac{L_A}{L_B} = 2$, $\dfrac{A_B}{A_A} = 1$, and $\dfrac{Y_A}{Y_B} = 0.5 \Rightarrow \dfrac{Y_B}{Y_A} = 2$.

Substituting the values into the ratio equation:

$\dfrac{\Delta L_A}{\Delta L_B} = \left(\dfrac{3Mg}{2Mg}\right) \times (2) \times (1) \times (2)$

$\dfrac{\Delta L_A}{\Delta L_B} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 4 = 6$

Answer: $6$

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of radius r made of a soft material of bulk modulus K is surrounded by a liquid in a cylindrical container. A massless piston of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> floats on the surface of the liquid, covering entire cross-section of cylindrical container. When a mass m is placed on the surface of the piston to compress the liquid, the fractional decrement in the radius of the sphere <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> , is:
question: A solid sphere of radius r made of a soft material of bulk modulus K is surrounded by a liquid in a cylindrical container. A massless piston of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> floats on the surface of the liquid, covering entire cross-section of cylindrical container. When a mass m is placed on the surface of the piston to compress the liquid, the fractional decrement in the radius of the sphere <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> , is:
solution:
For sphere,∨=43πr3⇒∆∨∨=3∆rr⇒P=KΔVV
⇒P=3KΔRR⇒mg3Ka=ΔRR.

subject: Physics
q: The velocity of water in a river is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> near the surface. If the river is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> deep, find the shearing stress between the horizontal layers of water. The coefficient of viscosity of water<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>poise</mi></math>.
question: The velocity of water in a river is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> near the surface. If the river is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> deep, find the shearing stress between the horizontal layers of water. The coefficient of viscosity of water<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>poise</mi></math>.
solution:
Viscous force =Fv=-ηA·dvdh

=10-3×A×18×10003600×5 ,   η=10-2 poiseη=10-3 decapoise mks

∴ FvA=185×3600=1200×5

⇒FvA=10-3 N m-2

subject: Physics
q: An aluminium rod with Young’s modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> undergoes elastic strain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The energy per unit volume stored in the rod in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>SI</mi></math> unit
question: An aluminium rod with Young’s modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> undergoes elastic strain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The energy per unit volume stored in the rod in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>SI</mi></math> unit
solution:
The data given is 

Y=7×1010 N m-2 and∆ll=0.04100

Young's modulus is given by the ratio of stress and strain,

Y=FA∆ll=FlA∆l

The energy stored is given as 

E=YA2l∆l2⇒E=YA2∆ll2×l   ...(i)

The energy per unit volume can be written from equation (i)

EV=Y2×∆ll2   ...(ii)

Substituting the values in equation (ii)

EV=12×7×1010×0.04×0.04104=56×102

q:
Four identical hollow cylindrical columns of mild steel support a big structure of mass 50×103 kg. The inner and outer radii of each column are 50 cm and 100 cm respectively. Assuming uniform local distribution, calculate the compression strain of each column.
[use Y=2.0×1011 Pa, g=9.8 m s-2]
question:
Four identical hollow cylindrical columns of mild steel support a big structure of mass 50×103 kg. The inner and outer radii of each column are 50 cm and 100 cm respectively. Assuming uniform local distribution, calculate the compression strain of each column.
[use Y=2.0×1011 Pa, g=9.8 m s-2]
solution:
ΔLL=mg4AY
=50×103×9.84π1002-502×10-4×2×1011=5×1054×π×150×50×10-4×2×1011
ΔLL=112π×10-5=2.60×10-7

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped at one end. If its other end is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. If the radius of the wire and the applied force both are reduced to half of their original values keeping original length constant, the increase in length will become:
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped at one end. If its other end is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. If the radius of the wire and the applied force both are reduced to half of their original values keeping original length constant, the increase in length will become:
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire, in the first case, can be written as

Y=Fπr2lL   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

F=Yπr2×lL   ...2

For the second case, the Young's modulus is given by

Y=F/2πr2/4l'L   ...3

From equation (3), it follows that

F=Yl'L×2×πr24   ...4

From equations (2) and (4), it implies that

Yπr2×lL=Yl'L×2×πr24⇒l'=2l

question: Each of three blocks P, Q and R shown in figure has a mass of 3 kg. Each of the wire A and B has cross-sectional area 0.005 cm2 and Young's modulus 2×1011 N m-2. Neglecting friction, the longitudinal strain on wire B is _____ ×10-4. (Take g=10 m s-2)

Acceleration of the system, a=3g9=103 m s-2

Now, for R we can write

30-T1=3×a

⇒T1=20 N

Therefore, strain developed in wire B, 

 strain= stress Y=T1AY=200.005×10-42×1011

=2×10-4

q: The load versus elongation graphs for four wires of same length and made of the same material are shown in the figure. The thinnest wire is represented by the line
question: The load versus elongation graphs for four wires of same length and made of the same material are shown in the figure. The thinnest wire is represented by the line
solution: From the graph, it is clear that for the same value of load, elongation is maximum for wire $O A$. Hence $O A$ is the thinnest wire among the four wires.

subject: Physics
q:
A copper wire of length $3\text{ m}$ is stretched by $3\text{ mm}$ by applying an external force. The volume of the wire is $600 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^3$. The elastic potential energy stored in the wire in stretched condition would be _______ J.
(Given Young modulus of copper $= 1.1 \times 10^{11}\text{ N/m}^2$)
question:
A copper wire of length $3\text{ m}$ is stretched by $3\text{ mm}$ by applying an external force. The volume of the wire is $600 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^3$. The elastic potential energy stored in the wire in stretched condition would be _______ J.
(Given Young modulus of copper $= 1.1 \times 10^{11}\text{ N/m}^2$)
solution:
Given parameters:
$L = 3\text{ m}$
$\Delta L = 3 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}$
$V = 600 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^3$
$Y = 1.1 \times 10^{11}\text{ N/m}^2$

Strain is given by:
$\text{Strain} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 10^{-3}$

Elastic potential energy stored in the wire is:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{Stress} \times \text{Strain} \times \text{Volume}$

Since $\text{Stress} = Y \times \text{Strain}$:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times Y \times (\text{Strain})^2 \times V$

Substituting the values:
$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (1.1 \times 10^{11}) \times (10^{-3})^2 \times (600 \times 10^{-6})$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.1 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-6} \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.1 \times 600 \times 10^{-1}$

$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 66 = 33\text{ J}$

Answer: $33$

subject: Physics
q: A copper wire of length $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ and a steel wire of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ having equal cross-sectional areas are joined end to end. The composite wire is stretched by a certain load which stretches the copper wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the Young's modulii of copper and steel are respectively $1.0 \times 10^{11}$ $\mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and $2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, the total extension of the composite wire is :
question: A copper wire of length $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ and a steel wire of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ having equal cross-sectional areas are joined end to end. The composite wire is stretched by a certain load which stretches the copper wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the Young's modulii of copper and steel are respectively $1.0 \times 10^{11}$ $\mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ and $2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, the total extension of the composite wire is :
solution:
$\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{c}} \times\left(\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}} / \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)=\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{s}} \times\left(\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}} / \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$
$$
\Rightarrow 1 \times 10^{11} \times\left(\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1}\right)=2 \times 10^{11} \times\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}}}{0.5}\right)
$$

$$
\therefore \Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=0.25 \mathrm{~mm}
$$
Therefore, total extension of the composite
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { wire }=\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}}+\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}} \\
& =1 \mathrm{~mm}+0.25 \mathrm{~m}=1.25 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
The diameter of a wire measured by a screw gauge of least count $0.001$ cm is $0.08$ cm. The length measured by a scale of least count $0.1$ cm is $150$ cm. When a weight of $100$ N is applied to the wire, the extension in length is $0.5$ cm, measured by a micrometer of least count $0.001$ cm. The error in the measured Young's modulus is $\alpha \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Ignore the contribution of the load to Young's modulus error calculation)
question:
The diameter of a wire measured by a screw gauge of least count $0.001$ cm is $0.08$ cm. The length measured by a scale of least count $0.1$ cm is $150$ cm. When a weight of $100$ N is applied to the wire, the extension in length is $0.5$ cm, measured by a micrometer of least count $0.001$ cm. The error in the measured Young's modulus is $\alpha \times 10^9$ N/m$^2$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Ignore the contribution of the load to Young's modulus error calculation)
solution:
The formula for Young's modulus is given by:

$Y = \dfrac{F L_0}{A \Delta L} = \dfrac{4 F L_0}{\pi d^2 \Delta L}$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$Y = \dfrac{4 \times 100 \times 1.5}{\pi \times (0.08 \times 10^{-2})^2 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-2})}$

$Y = \dfrac{600}{\pi \times 64 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{15}{8\pi} \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$

The relative error in Young's modulus is:

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{\Delta L_0}{L_0} + 2\dfrac{\Delta d}{d} + \dfrac{\Delta (\Delta L)}{\Delta L}$

Substituting the least counts as absolute errors and the measured values:

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{0.1}{150} + 2 \times \dfrac{0.001}{0.08} + \dfrac{0.001}{0.5}$

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{1}{1500} + \dfrac{1}{40} + \dfrac{1}{500}$

$\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \dfrac{2 + 75 + 6}{3000} = \dfrac{83}{3000}$

The absolute error in Young's modulus is:

$\Delta Y = Y \times \dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y} = \left(\dfrac{15}{8\pi} \times 10^{11}\right) \times \dfrac{83}{3000}$

$\Delta Y = \dfrac{15 \times 83}{24000 \pi} \times 10^{11} = \dfrac{83}{1600 \pi} \times 10^{11} = \dfrac{83}{16\pi} \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$\Delta Y = \dfrac{83}{16 \times 3.14} \times 10^9 = \dfrac{83}{50.24} \times 10^9 \approx 1.65 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$

Comparing this with $\alpha \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, we get $\alpha = 1.65$.

Answer: $1.65$

q: Young’s moduli of the material of wires A and B are in the ratio of 1 : 4, while its area of cross sections are in the ratio of 1 : 3. If the same amount of load is applied to both the wires, the amount of elongation produced in the wires A and B will be in the ratio of [Assume length of wires A and B are same]
question: Young’s moduli of the material of wires A and B are in the ratio of 1 : 4, while its area of cross sections are in the ratio of 1 : 3. If the same amount of load is applied to both the wires, the amount of elongation produced in the wires A and B will be in the ratio of [Assume length of wires A and B are same]
solution:
The formula to calculate the elongation of a metallic wire because of the application of a load can be written as

ΔL=FLAY   ...1

The ratio of the elongations for both the wires is, then, calculated as follows-

∆L1∆L2=FLA1Y1FLA2Y2= A2Y2A1Y1=31×41=121

q: Two blocks of mass 2 kg and 4 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley as shown in figure. The radius of wire is 4.0×10-5 m and Young's modulus of the metal is 2.0×1011 N m-2. The longitudinal strain developed in the wire is 1απ. The value of α is _____. [Use g=10 m s-2)
question: Two blocks of mass 2 kg and 4 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley as shown in figure. The radius of wire is 4.0×10-5 m and Young's modulus of the metal is 2.0×1011 N m-2. The longitudinal strain developed in the wire is 1απ. The value of α is _____. [Use g=10 m s-2)
solution:
For the given system, T=2m1m2m1+m2g=803 N

Area of the wire, A=πr2=16π×10−10 m2

Strain =Δll=FAY=TAY

=80316π×10−10×2×1011=112π

⇒α=12

subject: Physics
q: A certain pressure '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>' is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> litre of water and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> litre of a liquid separately. Water gets compressed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo>%</mo></math> whereas the liquid gets compressed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The ratio of Bulk modulus of water to that of the liquid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: A certain pressure '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>' is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> litre of water and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> litre of a liquid separately. Water gets compressed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo>%</mo></math> whereas the liquid gets compressed to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The ratio of Bulk modulus of water to that of the liquid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Bulk modulus is given by B=-ΔPΔVV

Now, in case of water,  Bwater=-ΔPΔVV=-ΔP0.01100

In case of liquid, Bliquid=-ΔP0.03100

Thus, the ratio BwaterBliquid=-ΔP0.0001-ΔP0.0003=3

Hence, the value of x=1.

q: A metal wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has breaking stress <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached at one end of the string and is rotating in a horizontal circle. The maximum linear velocity of block will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A metal wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has breaking stress <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached at one end of the string and is rotating in a horizontal circle. The maximum linear velocity of block will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Here in horizontal circular motion, the only centripetal force is tension. So,T=mω2r=mv2l=10×v20.5=20v2

Now, Tmax= Breaking stress × Area

⇒20v2=5×108×10-4

⇒20v2=5×104

Thus, the maximum linear velocity of block is 

v=14104=50 m s-1

q: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is projected from a rubber catapult of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretched by an amount <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The velocity of the projected stone is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Young's modulus of rubber <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is projected from a rubber catapult of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretched by an amount <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The velocity of the projected stone is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Young's modulus of rubber <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
By energy conservation
12·YAL·x2=12mv2
0.5×109×10-6×(0.04)20.1=201000v2
∴ v2=400
v=20 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
question: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
solution:
Elastic energy $=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{x}$
$$
\mathrm{F}=200 \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{x}=1 \mathrm{~mm}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \quad \therefore \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}=0.1 \mathrm{~J}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The Young's modulus of steel wire of radius $r$ and length $L$ is $Y$. If the radius $r$ and length $L$ of the wire are doubled then the value of $Y$
question: The Young's modulus of steel wire of radius $r$ and length $L$ is $Y$. If the radius $r$ and length $L$ of the wire are doubled then the value of $Y$
solution:
Young's modulus $Y$ is an intrinsic property of the material of the wire.

It depends only on the nature of the material and its temperature, and is independent of the dimensions of the wire such as length $L$ and radius $r$.

Therefore, when the radius and length of the steel wire are doubled, its Young's modulus remains unchanged.

Answer: remains unchanged

q: The normal density of a material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and its bulk modulus of elasticity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>. The magnitude of increase in density of material, when a pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied uniformly on all sides, will be :
question: The normal density of a material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> and its bulk modulus of elasticity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math>. The magnitude of increase in density of material, when a pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied uniformly on all sides, will be :
solution:
ρ=MV

dρρ=-dVV

k=-PdVV

-dVV=Pk

dρρ=Pk⇒dρ=ρPk

=1.44×109 N m-2

q: A uniform metallic wire is elongated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> when subjected to a linear force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The elongation, if its length and diameter is doubled and subjected to the same force will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A uniform metallic wire is elongated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> when subjected to a linear force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The elongation, if its length and diameter is doubled and subjected to the same force will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
F=Y.A·Δll⇒Δl=FY.A.·l⇒Δl=F.lY.πr2⇒Δl∝lr2

Δl2Δl1=l2l1r1r22=2122⇒Δl2Δl1=12⇒Δl2=Δl12

=0.042=0.02 m⇒Δl2=2 cm.

subject: Physics
q: With rise in temperature, the Young's modulus of elasticity
question: With rise in temperature, the Young's modulus of elasticity
solution:
With rise in temperature, Young’s modulus of  elasticity decreases due to the increase in atomic 
vibrations which leads to decrease in atomic forces. The Young's modulus is a measure of stiffness of the material. With rise in temperature, the length of the material increases which decreases its stiffness.
